items.note: Some phrases end with the preposition *to*. These phrases are also followed by the -ing form, not an infinitive.
The -ing form can also function as the subject of a sentence.

sections.title: Be careful
items: Some verbs (let, make) are typically followed by an object + bare infinitive (without to).
When make is used in the passive, it is followed by a full infinitive: The students were made to rewrite their essays.
Some verbs can take either the infinitive alone or object + full infinitive: She needs him to explain the problem.

sections.title: Meaning changes
rows: recall something that happened in the past
not forget to carry out a planned task
be unable to recall a past event or action
fail to do something that was planned
cease doing something permanently or for a time
pause in order to do something else
attempt something as an experiment or solution
make an effort or attempt to do something

exercises.title: A: -ing or infinitive
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is love + -ing, so the correct answer is "painting".

wrongOptions.paint: The bare infinitive (paint) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (paint) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

exercises.title: C: Meaning changes
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct form to complete each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct form to complete each sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: I forgot ...... him at the party last year.
items.originalSentence: I forgot ...... him at the party last year. **meeting / to meet**
items.completedSentence: I forgot ...... him at the party last year. meeting

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "meeting" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "meeting" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "to meet" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.to meet: "to meet" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.to meet: "to meet" doesn't fit here → "meeting" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: Don't forget ...... the lights before you leave.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "to switch off" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "switching off" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

optionContrast.to become: "to become" doesn't fit here → "to become" does, because The context points to "to become" — helps + infinitive.
optionContrast.D: "became" doesn't fit here → "to become" does, because it does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to become" is correct.
optionContrast.became: "became" doesn't fit here → "to become" does, because it does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to become" is correct.

exercises.title: F: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.

items.sentence:
I have a memory of visiting that museum. **REMEMBER**
I ...... that museum.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past memory, so the correct answer is "remember visiting".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "remember visiting", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Don't forget that you must lock the door. **REMEMBER**
...... the door.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future duty, so the correct answer is "Remember to lock".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "Remember to lock", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
We paused our game in order to answer the phone. **STOPPED**
We ...... the phone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is pause for purpose, so the correct answer is "stopped to answer".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "stopped to answer", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I experimented with fixing the problem by turning the computer off. **TRY**
...... the computer off.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is experiment, so the correct answer is "I tried turning".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "I tried turning", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The teacher forced us to stay after school. **MADE**
The teacher ...... after school.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Made us stay (bare infinitive).
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
They forced him to apologise. **MADE**
He ...... apologise.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive, so the correct answer is "was made to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "was made to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.
